fever.name: Fever
fever.description: Fever is a temporary increase in body temperature, often caused by an illness. A temperature above 38°C (100.4°F) is generally considered a fever. It is the body's natural response to fighting infections.
common_causes: Viral infections such as flu or common cold
Bacterial infections such as pneumonia, UTI, or strep throat
home_care: Rest and get plenty of sleep
Stay hydrated — drink water, clear broths, or electrolyte drinks
Take paracetamol or ibuprofen as directed on the packaging
Use a cool (not cold) damp cloth on the forehead
Keep the room cool and comfortable
Wear light clothing and avoid heavy blankets
Monitor temperature regularly with a thermometer
warning_signs: Temperature above 39.5°C (103°F) in adults
Fever lasting more than 3 days
Stiff neck, sensitivity to light, or severe headache
Rash that does not fade when pressed
Difficulty breathing
Confusion or unusual drowsiness
Fever in infants under 3 months
fever.when_to_see_doctor: See a doctor if your fever is above 39.5°C, lasts more than 3 days, or is accompanied by a stiff neck, rash, confusion, or difficulty breathing. Always seek urgent care for fever in infants under 3 months old.
follow_up_questions: How long have you had the fever?
Do you have any other symptoms like rash or stiff neck?
Have you recently traveled abroad?

headache.name: Headache
headache.description: Headaches are one of the most common pain conditions worldwide. They range from mild tension headaches to severe migraines. Understanding the type and triggers can help with effective management.
common_causes: Eyestrain from screens or poor vision
symptoms: Pain in the head, scalp, or neck
Pressure or tightening around the head
Pain that worsens with physical activity
home_care: Rest in a quiet, dark room
Drink plenty of water — dehydration is a common cause
Apply a cold or warm compress to the forehead or neck
Take over-the-counter pain relievers like paracetamol or ibuprofen
Practice relaxation techniques such as deep breathing or meditation
Limit screen time and take regular breaks
Maintain a regular sleep schedule
warning_signs: Sudden, severe 'thunderclap' headache — worst of your life
Headache with fever, stiff neck, or rash
Headache after head injury
Headache with confusion, vision changes, or weakness
Progressively worsening headache over days
Headache with one-sided weakness or speech problems
headache.when_to_see_doctor: Seek immediate care for a sudden severe headache, headache after head trauma, or headache with neurological symptoms like confusion, weakness, or vision loss. See a doctor if headaches are frequent or interfering with daily life.
follow_up_questions: How would you describe the pain — throbbing, pressure, or stabbing?
Do you have any visual changes, nausea, or sensitivity to light?
Have you had this type of headache before, or is this new?

chest_pain.name: Chest Pain
chest_pain.description: Chest pain can range from a minor muscle strain to a serious cardiac emergency. It is critical to assess accompanying symptoms carefully. Any chest pain with shortness of breath, arm pain, or sweating must be treated as a medical emergency until proven otherwise.
common_causes: Angina (reduced blood flow to the heart)
Muscle strain in the chest wall
Pulmonary embolism (blood clot in the lung)
symptoms: Pain, pressure, or tightness in the chest
Pain radiating to the left arm, jaw, neck, or back
home_care: If cardiac cause is suspected, call emergency services immediately — do not manage at home
Sit or lie down and rest in a comfortable position
Loosen tight clothing
Chew an aspirin (325mg) if not allergic and cardiac cause is suspected
For muscle strain: rest and apply ice or heat
For heartburn: antacids and avoid lying flat after eating
Stay calm and breathe slowly
warning_signs: Chest pain with left arm, jaw, or shoulder pain
Chest pain with sweating, nausea, or shortness of breath
Sudden severe chest pain
Chest pain in someone with known heart disease
Chest pain with rapid or irregular heartbeat
Chest pain with coughing up blood
chest_pain.when_to_see_doctor: Any chest pain that is new, severe, or accompanied by shortness of breath, sweating, or arm/jaw pain requires emergency evaluation immediately. Do not drive yourself — call emergency services.
follow_up_questions: Does the pain spread to your arm, jaw, or shoulder?
Are you sweating, feeling sick, or short of breath?
Do you have a history of heart problems?

hand_pain.name: Hand Pain
hand_pain.description: Hand pain can arise from many causes — from muscle overuse and poor circulation to nerve compression and serious cardiac events. Left hand pain in particular deserves special attention as it can be an early warning sign of cardiac issues, especially when combined with chest discomfort, sweating, or shortness of breath.
common_causes: Muscle strain or overuse from repetitive activities
Dehydration causing electrolyte imbalance and muscle cramps
Carpal tunnel syndrome (nerve compression at the wrist)
Nerve compression in the neck or shoulder (referred pain)
Cardiac referral — left arm and hand pain can be a sign of heart problems
symptoms: Pain, aching, or cramping in the hand or fingers
Weakness when gripping or holding objects
Swelling or redness in the joints
Coldness or colour change in the fingers
Muscle spasms in the hand or forearm
Pain that worsens with activity or particular positions
home_care: Rest the affected hand and avoid repetitive strain
Gently stretch and flex the fingers and wrist
Apply a warm compress to relax muscles and improve circulation
Stay well hydrated — dehydration commonly causes hand cramps
Take electrolytes (potassium, magnesium, calcium) if cramping is present
Use an ergonomic setup if the pain is work-related
Over-the-counter pain relievers such as ibuprofen for inflammation
For carpal tunnel: wear a wrist splint at night
warning_signs: Left hand pain combined with chest pain or pressure
Left hand numbness with sweating, nausea, or shortness of breath
Sudden severe pain with no clear cause
Hand pain with signs of poor circulation (blue/white fingers)
Pain after injury with visible deformity
Rapidly spreading swelling or redness suggesting infection
hand_pain.when_to_see_doctor: Seek emergency care immediately if left hand pain is accompanied by chest pain, sweating, jaw pain, or shortness of breath — these may indicate a heart attack. See a doctor for persistent pain, numbness, weakness, or if home care does not help within a few days.
follow_up_questions: Is the pain in your left hand or right hand, and does it extend up your arm?
Do you also have chest pain, sweating, or shortness of breath?
Did the pain come on suddenly or gradually?

leg_pain.name: Leg Pain
leg_pain.description: Leg pain is extremely common and can result from muscle fatigue, poor circulation, nerve issues, or joint problems. Calf pain with swelling can occasionally indicate a deep vein thrombosis (DVT), which requires prompt medical assessment.
common_causes: Muscle overuse, strain, or exercise soreness
Dehydration and electrolyte imbalance causing cramps
Sciatica (nerve pain from the lower back)
Arthritis affecting the knee or hip
symptoms: Aching or cramping in the calf, thigh, or shin
Swelling of the lower leg or ankle
Numbness or tingling down the leg
Redness or warmth over the leg
Night cramps that wake you from sleep
home_care: Rest and elevate the leg
Apply ice for acute injury or heat for muscle cramps
Gently stretch the calf and thigh muscles
Stay hydrated and replenish electrolytes
Over-the-counter pain relief such as ibuprofen or paracetamol
Gentle massage for muscle cramps
Compression stockings for swelling or varicose veins
warning_signs: Calf pain with swelling, redness, and warmth — possible DVT
Sudden severe leg pain with pale or cold skin — possible arterial blockage
Leg pain with chest pain or shortness of breath
Numbness or weakness in both legs
Pain after recent long flight or bed rest (DVT risk)
leg_pain.when_to_see_doctor: Seek urgent care if you have calf pain with swelling, warmth, and redness (possible DVT). See a doctor for persistent leg pain, pain with walking, or any neurological symptoms like numbness or weakness.
follow_up_questions: Where exactly is the pain — calf, thigh, knee, or all over?
Is the leg swollen, red, or warm to the touch?
Did the pain start after exercise, or has it been gradual?

stomach_pain.name: Stomach Pain
stomach_pain.description: Stomach and abdominal pain can be caused by a wide variety of conditions, from mild indigestion to serious conditions requiring surgery. The location, nature, and accompanying symptoms help determine the likely cause.
common_causes: Gas, bloating, or irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
Kidney stones or urinary tract infection
symptoms: Cramping or sharp pain in the abdomen
Fever with abdominal pain (may indicate infection)
home_care: Rest and avoid eating until nausea settles
Stay hydrated with small sips of water or clear fluids
Eat bland foods such as toast, rice, or crackers
Avoid dairy, fatty, or spicy foods until better
Use a heating pad on the abdomen for cramps
Over-the-counter antacids for heartburn or acid reflux
Peppermint tea can help with gas and bloating
warning_signs: Severe sudden pain in the lower right abdomen (possible appendicitis)
Abdomen rigid or board-like to touch
Vomiting blood or black/tarry stools
Severe pain with high fever
Pain after abdominal injury
Signs of dehydration from prolonged vomiting or diarrhea
stomach_pain.when_to_see_doctor: Seek emergency care for sudden severe abdominal pain, especially in the lower right. See a doctor promptly for pain lasting more than a few hours, accompanied by fever, vomiting blood, or if you cannot keep fluids down.
follow_up_questions: Where exactly is the pain, and does it move anywhere?
Do you have any vomiting, diarrhea, or fever alongside the pain?
Did the pain start suddenly or come on gradually?

diabetes.name: Diabetes
diabetes.description: Diabetes is a chronic condition affecting how the body processes blood glucose. Type 1 is autoimmune, while Type 2 is largely lifestyle-related. Both require careful management. Uncontrolled blood sugar can cause serious complications.
common_causes: Type 1: Autoimmune destruction of insulin-producing cells
Type 2: Insulin resistance due to lifestyle, obesity, genetics
Hormonal disorders such as Cushing's syndrome
symptoms: Tingling or numbness in hands and feet
Darkened skin in neck/armpits (acanthosis nigricans, Type 2)
home_care: Monitor blood sugar levels regularly as advised
Follow a balanced diet low in refined carbohydrates and sugar
Exercise regularly — helps improve insulin sensitivity
Take medications or insulin exactly as prescribed
Stay hydrated
Check feet daily for wounds, as healing is slower
Maintain healthy weight for Type 2 management
warning_signs: Blood sugar very high (above 15 mmol/L or 270 mg/dL) with nausea and vomiting
Blood sugar very low (below 4 mmol/L or 70 mg/dL) with confusion or shaking
Fruity breath, deep breathing, confusion — possible diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA)
Severe confusion or loss of consciousness
Chest pain or signs of diabetic emergency
diabetes.when_to_see_doctor: Seek emergency care for signs of DKA (fruity breath, severe nausea, confusion) or severe hypoglycemia (shaking, confusion, unconsciousness). See your doctor regularly for diabetes management, and immediately if glucose is very high or very low.
follow_up_questions: Do you have a known diagnosis of diabetes, and are you on medication?
What are your current blood sugar readings?
Are you experiencing excessive thirst, frequent urination, or blurred vision?

blood_pressure.name: Blood Pressure
blood_pressure.description: Blood pressure is the force of blood against artery walls. High blood pressure (hypertension) is above 140/90 mmHg and is a major risk factor for heart disease and stroke. Low blood pressure (hypotension) can cause dizziness and fainting.
common_causes: High BP: Poor diet, high salt intake, sedentary lifestyle
High BP: Stress, obesity, smoking, alcohol
High BP: Kidney disease, hormonal disorders
Low BP: Heart problems or hormonal issues
Low BP: Prolonged bed rest or standing up quickly (orthostatic hypotension)
symptoms: High BP: Often no symptoms (the silent killer)
High BP: Headache, nosebleed, dizziness in severe cases
Low BP: Dizziness or lightheadedness when standing
Low BP: Blurred vision, fatigue, weakness
home_care: For high BP: Reduce salt, alcohol, and processed food intake
For high BP: Exercise regularly and maintain healthy weight
For high BP: Manage stress with relaxation techniques
For high BP: Take prescribed medications consistently
For low BP: Increase fluid and salt intake (if advised)
For low BP: Rise slowly from sitting or lying positions
For low BP: Wear compression stockings if recommended
warning_signs: BP above 180/120 mmHg (hypertensive crisis)
Severe headache with very high BP
Chest pain or shortness of breath with high BP
BP below 90/60 with confusion or loss of consciousness
Sudden drop in BP with signs of shock
blood_pressure.when_to_see_doctor: Seek emergency care if BP is above 180/120 mmHg, especially with chest pain or confusion. See a doctor regularly if you have hypertension. Seek same-day care if BP drops cause fainting or persistent symptoms.
follow_up_questions: Do you know your current blood pressure reading?
Are you on any blood pressure medications?
Do you feel dizzy when you stand up, or is the dizziness constant?

dehydration.name: Dehydration
dehydration.description: Dehydration occurs when the body loses more fluids than it takes in. Even mild dehydration can cause muscle cramps, headaches, and fatigue. Severe dehydration is a medical emergency, especially in children and the elderly.
common_causes: Not drinking enough water throughout the day
Excessive sweating from heat or exercise
Vomiting and diarrhea causing rapid fluid loss
Hot weather or vigorous physical activity
symptoms: Muscle cramps, especially in the hands and legs
Confusion or irritability in severe cases
home_care: Drink water steadily — aim for 8 glasses per day in normal conditions
Use oral rehydration salts (ORS) if vomiting or diarrhea is present
Sports drinks or electrolyte drinks to replenish minerals
Eat water-rich foods such as cucumber, watermelon, or oranges
Rest in a cool environment if overheated
Avoid alcohol and caffeine which increase fluid loss
Increase intake if exercising, sweating heavily, or in hot weather
warning_signs: Confusion or extreme irritability
No urination for 8 or more hours
Rapid heartbeat with very low blood pressure
Sunken eyes or very dry skin that does not bounce back
Fainting or loss of consciousness
Severe muscle cramps that do not resolve with hydration
dehydration.when_to_see_doctor: Seek emergency care for severe dehydration with confusion, rapid heartbeat, fainting, or no urination. See a doctor if unable to keep fluids down, if dehydration is in a young child or elderly person, or if symptoms do not improve with oral rehydration.
follow_up_questions: How much water have you had today, and do you feel thirsty?
Have you had vomiting, diarrhea, or excessive sweating recently?
Are you experiencing muscle cramps or dizziness alongside the thirst?

muscle_cramps.name: Muscle Cramps
muscle_cramps.description: Muscle cramps are sudden, involuntary contractions of one or more muscles. They can be intensely painful and are very common. Most cramps are benign and related to dehydration, electrolyte imbalance, overuse, or poor circulation — but persistent or severe cramps should be evaluated.
common_causes: Dehydration — reduced fluid causes impaired muscle function
Electrolyte imbalance — low potassium, magnesium, calcium, or sodium
Overuse or prolonged exercise without adequate stretching
Poor blood circulation to the muscles
Mineral deficiency, especially in people with poor diet
Prolonged sitting or standing in one position
symptoms: Sudden sharp or gripping pain in a muscle
Visible or palpable muscle hardness or knot
Temporary inability to use the affected muscle
Cramping that worsens during or after exercise
Twitching or spasms before or after the main cramp
Cramps most common in calves, feet, and hands
Night-time cramps that wake you from sleep
home_care: Stretch the affected muscle gently and hold for 30 seconds
Hydrate immediately — drink water or an electrolyte drink
Take electrolytes: potassium (bananas, avocado), magnesium (nuts, dark leafy greens), calcium (dairy, leafy greens)
Apply a warm compress or hot water bottle to relax the muscle
Gentle massage to the cramping area
For cold cramps, apply an ice pack to reduce inflammation
Rest the muscle and avoid overuse
Ensure adequate warm-up and cool-down around exercise
warning_signs: Muscle cramps with left hand or arm pain AND chest pain (cardiac alert)
Severe cramps with confusion, rapid heartbeat, or fainting
Cramps that do not resolve with stretching and hydration
Frequent cramps with weakness or significant fatigue
Cramps in someone with liver disease or on dialysis
Cramps following significant fluid loss or illness
muscle_cramps.when_to_see_doctor: See a doctor if cramps are frequent, severe, or not relieved by stretching and hydration. Seek urgent care if cramps occur alongside chest pain, left arm pain, or sweating — this combination may indicate a cardiac event. Also seek care if cramps are associated with significant weakness or follow illness.
follow_up_questions: How long have you been experiencing the cramps, and where are they located?
Have you been drinking enough water and eating well today?
Do you also have any chest pain, shortness of breath, or dizziness?

cold_flu.name: Cold & Flu
cold_flu.description: The common cold and influenza (flu) are viral respiratory illnesses. Colds are generally milder and mainly affect the nose and throat, while flu tends to come on suddenly and includes fever, body aches, and significant fatigue. Both are highly contagious.
common_causes: Rhinoviruses — the most common cause of colds
symptoms: Mild to moderate fever (more common in flu)
Fatigue and body aches (more prominent in flu)
home_care: Rest at home and avoid spreading to others
Stay well hydrated — water, hot tea, or broth
Honey and lemon drink for sore throat and cough
Use saline nasal spray or rinse for congestion
Paracetamol or ibuprofen for fever and aches
Steam inhalation to relieve congestion
Throat lozenges for sore throat
Elevate your head while sleeping to ease congestion
warning_signs: Difficulty breathing or rapid breathing
High persistent fever above 39.5°C for more than 3 days
Worsening symptoms after initial improvement (secondary infection)
Chest pain or persistent coughing up blood
Severe headache with stiff neck
Confusion or difficulty waking
cold_flu.when_to_see_doctor: See a doctor if symptoms are severe, worsen after 7-10 days, or if you have difficulty breathing, high persistent fever, or are in a high-risk group (elderly, immunocompromised, pregnant). Antiviral medications may help if flu is diagnosed early.
follow_up_questions: Do you have a fever, and have you measured your temperature?
How long have you had these symptoms, and are they getting worse?
Do you have any difficulty breathing or chest pain?

first_aid.name: First Aid
first_aid.description: First aid covers the immediate care given to an injured or ill person before professional medical help is available. Proper first aid can prevent conditions from worsening and can sometimes save lives.
common_causes: Cuts and lacerations from sharp objects
Burns from heat, chemicals, or electricity
Sprains and strains from sudden movements
symptoms: Swelling and bruising at the injury site
Redness, blistering, or charring with burns
Inability to move a limb normally (possible fracture)
Signs of infection: pus, warmth, increased redness after a few days
home_care: Cuts: Clean with cool running water, apply pressure to stop bleeding, cover with sterile bandage
Burns: Cool under running water for 20 minutes, do not use ice or butter
Sprains: Use RICE — Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation
Bruises: Apply ice pack wrapped in cloth for 15-20 minutes
Nosebleed: Sit forward, pinch soft part of nose for 10-15 minutes
Insect stings: Remove stinger, apply cold compress
Choking (adult): Encourage coughing, then back blows and abdominal thrusts
warning_signs: Heavy bleeding that does not stop after 15 minutes of pressure
Deep wound exposing bone, tendon, or tissue
Burns larger than 5cm or on the face, hands, or genitals
Signs of fracture with visible deformity or complete inability to move
Signs of infection — pus, red streaks, worsening swelling after 48 hours
Severe allergic reaction to an insect sting
first_aid.when_to_see_doctor: Seek emergency care for severe bleeding, deep wounds, large burns, suspected fractures, or signs of anaphylaxis. Visit a doctor or urgent care clinic for wounds that may need stitches, signs of infection, or burns to sensitive areas.
follow_up_questions: What type of injury occurred — cut, burn, sprain, or something else?
Is the bleeding controlled, or is it still heavy?
Is there any chance of a broken bone, or can you move the area?

emergency.name: Medical Emergency
emergency.description: A medical emergency is a sudden, serious injury or illness that threatens a person's life or could cause long-term harm if not treated immediately. Recognising the signs and acting quickly — including calling emergency services — can save lives.
common_causes: Severe asthma attack or respiratory failure
symptoms: Chest pain with sweating and arm pain
Face drooping, arm weakness, speech difficulties (stroke)
Signs of overdose — pinpoint pupils, limp body, slow breathing
home_care: Call emergency services (911, 112, or 999) immediately
Stay on the line with the dispatcher and follow their instructions
For cardiac arrest: begin CPR — 30 chest compressions, 2 rescue breaths
For anaphylaxis: use EpiPen if available, then call emergency services
For seizure: protect from injury, do not restrain or put anything in the mouth
For severe bleeding: apply firm direct pressure
For unconscious but breathing: place in recovery position
Do not give food or water to an unconscious person
warning_signs: Any loss of consciousness
Absent or gasping breathing
No pulse or very weak pulse
Uncontrolled heavy bleeding
Sudden confusion, paralysis, or inability to speak
emergency.when_to_see_doctor: Call emergency services (911/112/999) immediately. Do not wait or drive to hospital — paramedics can begin treatment en route.
follow_up_questions: Is the person conscious and breathing?
Have you called emergency services?
What happened — was there a collapse, injury, or sudden illness?

emergency_combinations.name: Possible Heart Attack
emergency_combinations.message: These symptoms together — especially chest pain, left arm/hand pain, sweating — can be signs of a heart attack.
emergency_combinations.action: Call emergency services (911/112/999) immediately. Do not drive yourself. Chew an aspirin (325mg) if not allergic while waiting for help.

emergency_combinations.name: Possible Stroke (FAST)
emergency_combinations.message: These symptoms may indicate a stroke. Use FAST: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call.
emergency_combinations.action: Call emergency services immediately. Note the exact time symptoms started.

emergency_combinations.name: Breathing Emergency
emergency_combinations.message: Severe difficulty breathing is a medical emergency.

emergency_combinations.name: Severe Allergic Reaction
emergency_combinations.message: Throat swelling can block your airway — this is life-threatening.
emergency_combinations.action: Use EpiPen if available. Call emergency services immediately.
